main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrots, peas, potatoes, cauliflower, bell peppers)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Veg Jaipuri weighs approximately 400 g, typically served as a main dish alongside rice or Indian bread such as roti or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Veg Jaipuri as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for mixed cooked vegetables and vegetable curry preparations, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for individual vegetable components (carrots, peas, potatoes, tomatoes, onions) and cooking oil. The input record values (energy 112.5 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 11.25 g) are consistent with this adapted profile and are retained with medium confidence. Potassium estimated at 320 mg/100g based on ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable data. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not available in ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Jaipuri. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The dish contains potatoes (GI ~70–80 for boiled potato) diluted by lower-GI vegetables (carrots GI ~35, peas GI ~48, cauliflower GI ~15) and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100g), yielding a blended estimate of approximately 55. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 45.0) / 100 = 24.75, rounded to 24.8 for a 400 g serving (45.0 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 200 mg/100g; 800 mg per standard 400 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving contributes 800 mg sodium, representing approximately 35–40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertension management; patients should request reduced-salt preparation and pair with potassium-rich foods such as the dish's own mixed vegetables (estimated 320 mg potassium/100g) to support DASH-aligned sodium-potassium balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI 55 (low); glycemic load 24.8 per 400 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of 24.8 per standard serving place this dish in the moderate concern category for Type 2 Diabetes; patients should request reduced potato content or substitution with lower-GI vegetables such as cauliflower, and avoid pairing with large portions of white rice or refined bread to keep the overall meal glycemic load below 20.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.25 g/100g; trans fat 0.03 g/100g; cholesterol 0 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 1.25 g/100g (5.0 g per 400 g serving) is within the AHA recommended limit of less than 13 g/day for a 2,000 kcal diet, cholesterol is absent, and the vegetable-dominant composition with turmeric and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100g) supports cardiovascular health; the primary caution is sodium at 800 mg per serving, which should be managed by requesting reduced-salt preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger); nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric and ginger in the Jaipuri masala provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, the dish contains nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, potatoes, bell peppers, green chillies) whose solanine content may aggravate symptoms in solanine-sensitive RA patients, who should request substitution of nightshades with non-nightshade vegetables such as carrots or zucchini.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Jaipuri is a North Indian vegetarian curry originating from Jaipur, the capital of Rajasthan, India. It is prepared by sautéing a medley of mixed vegetables — typically carrots, peas, potatoes, cauliflower, and bell peppers — with onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and green chillies in cooking oil, then simmering the mixture in a richly spiced gravy made with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The dish has a vibrant orange-red colour, a moderately spiced and aromatic flavour profile, and a semi-dry to semi-gravy texture depending on preparation. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 112.5 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 6.25 g fat (saturated fat 1.25 g), 11.25 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, 2.0 g sugar, and 200 mg sodium. The vegetable-dominant composition contributes vitamin C (10 mg/100g), iron (0.75 mg/100g), and an estimated 320 mg potassium/100g. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (800 mg per 400 g serving) warrants attention for hypertension patients, while the low estimated glycemic index of approximately 55 and glycemic load of 24.8 per serving require portion management for Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory spice blend makes it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health. In Singapore, Veg Jaipuri is commonly served at Indian restaurants and food courts as a main dish accompanied by roti, naan, or steamed rice.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Veg Jaipuri as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable and vegetable curry data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. The input record values are consistent with this adapted profile. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curry components. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite mixed vegetable curry preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this composite Rajasthani vegetable curry preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single composite entry as of 2026. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally absent. 5. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as Turkish with ethnic tags of Turkish, Kebab, and Shawarma; Veg Jaipuri is a North Indian (Rajasthani) dish and has been corrected accordingly. 6. Potassium estimated at 320 mg/100g based on ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable data; this is an estimate and not a directly measured value for this composite dish. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Veg Jaipuri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 8. GI of 55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database thresholds (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70).
